title: Review of Interventional Options and Surgical Techniques in the Management of Acute Pulmonary Embolism
authors: Chen Chia Wang, Kevin C. McGann, et al.
one_line_summary: Narrative review of interventional and surgical options for acute pulmonary embolism, focusing on surgical techniques, outcomes, and the evolving role of multidisciplinary pulmonary embolism response teams.
key_pearls: Surgical pulmonary embolectomy is indicated for centrally located high-risk PE when fibrinolytics are contraindicated, systemic or catheter-based therapy has failed, clot-in-transit is present, or a large PFO exists.
Contemporary surgical embolectomy operative mortality ranges from 7-16% in single-center and database studies, a significant improvement from historical rates of 87%.
VA-ECMO should be utilized early in high-risk PE with hemodynamic instability not improving with initial therapy, and can bridge patients to definitive reperfusion interventions.

Catheter-directed mechanical thrombectomy demonstrated superior composite outcomes (mortality, bleeding, ICU admission) compared to catheter-directed thrombolysis for intermediate-risk PE in the PEERLESS trial.
The most common postoperative complication after surgical embolectomy is prolonged ventilation, with rates up to 44.5% in large database reviews.

Pulmonary embolism response teams (PERTs) are associated with lower short-term mortality, reduced length of stay, and higher utilization of advanced therapies, though cost-effectiveness remains unclear.

Assessment of chronicity is crucial for surgical planning, as underlying chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension requires a more complex pulmonary endarterectomy limited to experienced, high-volume centers.
population: Patients with acute pulmonary embolism, including massive/high-risk and submassive/intermediate-risk classifications

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Pulmonary embolism (PE) is a form of venous thromboembolism in the pulmonary arterial vasculature, occurring either from a migrating clot or from in situ clot formation. In the United States, the annual incidence of PE was 393,000 in 2023, and the overall lifetime risk of PE at age 45 is roughly 8.1%. The most common classification schema for PE follows the guidelines by the American Heart Association (AHA) in 2011 and the European Society of Cardiology (ESC) in 2019. These societies created three tiers of PE based on clinical variables: (1) massive (AHA) or high-risk (ESC), (2) submassive (AHA) or intermediate risk (ESC), and (3) low risk (both AHA and ESC). Of note, the new 2026 AHA/ACC guidelines introduced five new tiers of risk stratification (A — E, with E being the most clinically severe). Given its recency, this review will refer to risk classes based on the prior 2011 AHA or 2019 ESC guidelines. Medical management and transcatheter interventions remain the mainstay for treatment, with surgical intervention reserved as a last resort. However, there have been increasing literature supporting the safety and efficacy of extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) and surgical intervention in acute high/intermediate risk PE. This surgically-focused review will outline the contemporary workup and management of PE, along with interventional and surgical management techniques and the associated outcomes and approach to potential complications.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.content: The pathophysiological basis of PE can be explained by the fundamental principles of Virchow's triad: venous stasis, endothelial injury, and a hypercoagulable state. Risk factors for PE can either be hereditary or acquired. Hereditary risk factors include hematologic conditions that disrupt a patient's intrinsic balance between clot formation and breakdown, such as factor V Leiden, protein S deficiency, and protein C deficiency. Acquired risk factors include, but are not limited to, antiphospholipid syndrome, malignancy, recent surgery, heart failure, and infection. Most cases of PE arise from emboli originating in lower extremity proximal veins, such as the iliac, femoral, and popliteal veins. Obstructive shock caused by acute PE stems from the sudden, large increase in right ventricular (RV) afterload due to a hemodynamically significant clot burden impeding pulmonary arterial flow. The RV is unable to rapidly compensate, leading to acute RV dilation with progression to RV failure if untreated. This obstruction also causes decreased left ventricular (LV) preload, which can lead to precipitous hemodynamic collapse manifested by systemic hypotension and shock. Furthermore, hypoxia-induced pulmonary vasoconstriction can further increase the pulmonary vascular resistance and exacerbate RV dysfunction and reduced cardiac output. Surgical pulmonary endarterectomy aims to remove this source of obstruction, but residual clots in branch arteries may lead to persistent pulmonary hypertension and continued RV deterioration.

sections.heading: Diagnosis
sections.content:
Clinical assessment: The clinical presentation of a patient with PE is wide-ranging and may vary from asymptomatic to cardiopulmonary arrest. Common symptoms and exam findings include dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, crackles on lung auscultation, and peripheral edema. Crucially, up to a third of patients may be asymptomatic or have only mild symptoms, even in the setting of a large PE. The Wells criteria qualifies a patient's risk of PE as high, moderate, or low probability based on variables such as tachycardia, recent immobilization or surgery, and previous venous thromboembolism. The Pulmonary Embolism Severity Index (PESI) score stratifies patients into five classes based on high-risk variables, with increasing classes suggestive of short term mortality and poor outcomes. These variables include age over 80, history of cancer, chronic cardiopulmonary disease, tachycardia, hypotension, and hypoxemia. A model by the ESC in 2014 combines the simplified PESI score with presence of RV dysfunction on imaging and troponin elevation to categorize patients into low, intermediate-low, intermediate-high, or high risk. However, rapid management and intervention should still be performed in cases of high-risk PE, regardless of PESI score.

Laboratory and electrocardiography findings: Laboratory measurements, electrocardiography (ECG), and imaging studies may aid in the clinical workup and decision-making when there is suspicion for PE, but alone are not diagnostic tests. Arterial blood gas (ABG) measurements are often abnormal, with the most common findings being hypoxemia (74%), greater alveolar-arterial gradient for oxygen (62-86%), and respiratory alkalosis and hypocapnia (41%). Even still, ABGs can be normal in up to 18% of patients with PE. D-dimer is not diagnostic due to a high false positive rate, but the absence of an elevated D-dimer can help exclude PE when the initial clinical suspicion is low. ECG abnormalities are common but also non-specific in PE and include tachycardia, ST-segment changes, right axis deviation, and T-wave changes.

Imaging modalities and findings: Common imaging studies obtained in the workup of PE include plain chest radiographs and echocardiograms. Chest radiographs are used to investigate alternative causes of a patient's symptoms, and a normal radiograph can be seen in 12-22% of patients with PE. Nonspecific findings include atelectasis and effusions. Other findings such as a Hampton hump, Westermark sign, and Fleischner or Palla sign raise suspicion for PE, but are rarely seen. Echocardiography, specifically transthoracic echocardiography (TTE), is a useful and quick method of risk stratification and management planning when suspicious of PE. RV dysfunction caused by acute PE may lead to signs such as RV hypokinesis, right atrial enlargement, increased RV end diastolic diameter, paradoxical septal motion, or direct visualization of thrombi in the right heart or pulmonary artery (PA). In PE patients with hemodynamic instability, signs of RV dysfunction on TTE are an indication for thrombolytic treatment. LV dysfunction at diagnosis is strongly associated with increased 30-d mortality. Biventricular dysfunction on TTE is an indicator of severe hemodynamic collapse. Given the relative speed and ease of use compared to more advanced imaging options, TTE can be deployed at bedside and is crucial for early identification of high-risk PEs to facilitate timely treatment. Serial TTE measurements can also help to identify progressive disease status that may necessitate escalation of interventions.

The gold standard diagnostic tool for PE is CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA). This is the first-line diagnostic test, with reported sensitivities and specificities for PE above 90% regardless of pretest probability. It can also help guide management by characterizing thrombus location, size, and any signs of right heart strain/enlargement. On CTPA, an increase in the right ventricle-to-left ventricle diameter ratio greater than 1 is highly suggestive of acute RV dysfunction and is predictive of all-cause and PE-related mortality. CTPA may also identify additional factors contributing to decreased cardiopulmonary reserve, such as pleural effusion, pneumothorax, or atelectasis. When CTPA is contraindicated (e.g., severe allergy to iodinated contrast or renal failure) or indeterminate, a ventilation perfusion (V/Q) scan is an alternative option. The findings of V/Q scans should be interpreted in the context of the pretest probability for PE, which is commonly quantified using Wells criteria. In patients with a normal V/Q scan, further testing is not needed, regardless of clinical probability. In patients with both a low-probability V/Q scan and low clinical probability (Wells score <2), no further testing is needed. In patients with both a high-probability V/Q scan and high clinical probability (Wells score >6), a diagnosis of PE can be made. All other combinations of V/Q scan findings and clinical probability are deemed indeterminate, and further testing is required. Crucially, V/Q scan is more sensitive than CTPA for follow-up studies to detect residual pulmonary vascular obstruction. Therefore, it serves as the gold standard screening test for residual pulmonary vascular obstruction after initial treatment that may necessitate further management. Additional diagnostic options include magnetic resonance pulmonary angiography and catheter-based pulmonary angiography. However, magnetic resonance is time consuming and burdensome and therefore is only used in select patients who cannot undergo CTPA and have indeterminate V/Q scans. Similarly, invasive angiography has fallen out of favor in face of quicker, noninvasive modalities, and is now mainly used as an adjunct tool in advanced invasive pharmacological or mechanical interventions.
section_pearls: CTPA is the first-line diagnostic test with sensitivities and specificities above 90% regardless of pretest probability.
An RV/LV diameter ratio >1 on CTPA is highly suggestive of acute RV dysfunction and predictive of all-cause and PE-related mortality.
V/Q scan is more sensitive than CTPA for detecting residual pulmonary vascular obstruction after initial treatment.

sections.heading: Pulmonary Embolism Response Teams
sections.content: Given the historically poor outcomes in the treatment of acute PE, there has been increasing adoption in the development of multidisciplinary teams known as pulmonary embolism response teams (PERTs) to help guide consensus for early treatment. These teams typically consist of specialists from cardiovascular medicine, pulmonary/critical care, interventional cardiology, interventional radiology, vascular surgery, cardiothoracic surgery, emergency medicine, hematology, and vascular medicine. At a minimum, PERTs should include physician representation from medicine, interventional cardiology, and cardiac surgery for collective decision making on the best treatment approaches, whether medical, catheter-based, or surgical. Mechanical support options are also typically discussed in patients who have high-risk features or are clinically decompensating. With the adoption of PERTs, data suggests improvement in patient outcomes, including lower lengths of stay, lower rates of major or clinically relevant bleeding, and higher utilization of advanced therapies such as transcatheter and surgical interventions. While a 2019 statement by the AHA determined that PERTs may not be cost-effective given the unclear effects on outcomes, a more recent meta-analysis in 2025 demonstrated lower short-term mortality in patients treated with PERT. Still, it remains unclear if the observed benefit in outcomes is driven by an increased usage of advanced therapies, which will be an important question to answer when evaluating the cost effectiveness of PERTs.
section_pearls: PERTs should include at minimum representation from medicine, interventional cardiology, and cardiac surgery.
A 2025 meta-analysis demonstrated lower short-term mortality in patients treated with PERT.

sections.heading: Medical Management
sections.content:
Initial management of PE includes fluid management with respiratory and hemodynamic support. Supplemental oxygen should be considered even in the absence of hypoxemia and mechanical ventilation in the setting of severe hypoxemia or pending cardiac/respiratory arrest. During mechanical ventilation, low tidal volumes are recommended to avoid further increasing pulmonary vascular resistance and RV dilatation. Noninvasive ventilation is preferred due to the risk of hemodynamic collapse during the intubation process for invasive ventilation, and special focus on avoiding hypotension and worsening hypoxemia is needed when invasive ventilation is necessary. The AHA specifically recommends invasive hemodynamic monitoring and preparation for mechanical circulatory support in patients requiring invasive ventilation. In patients that are normotensive with RV dysfunction associated with intermediate risk PE, administration of intravenous furosemide has been shown to improve RV function compared to volume expansion. This is particularly important when there is severe RV dilatation, as fluid administration could worsen the fluid backup, whereas diuretics may help reduce RV wall stress. However, in patients with a low central venous pressure demonstrated on echocardiography by evaluation of the inferior vena cava, a fluid challenge may help improve the cardiac index. For patients with gross volume overload, who appear to be preload dependent, vasopressors may be needed. Hemodynamic support includes norepinephrine as the first choice and vasopressin as an adjunct. In cases of low cardiac output despite vasopressor therapy, dobutamine may be considered to increase contractility. However, it should be used with caution as it could worsen ventilation-perfusion mismatch due to overperfusion of unobstructive pulmonary vessels and could exacerbate hypotension if used without a vasopressor. Epinephrine could be considered in severe shock and should be used if the patient has a cardiac arrest. The role of pulmonary vasodilators in acute PE remain controversial, although some data may suggest benefit if the patient has existing chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension. Veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (VA-ECMO) should be utilized early if prior interventions are not successful.

Even without a definitive diagnosis, empiric anticoagulation may be started depending on risk of bleeding, clinical suspicion for PE, patient hemodynamics, and expected time to a diagnostic test. For definitive therapy in confirmed PE, the decision between medical and surgical therapy is dependent on various factors, such as classification of the PE, patient bleeding risk, and value of thrombolysis. In patients who do not have a high risk of bleeding, medical management options such as thrombolytic therapy and anticoagulation are used. In intermediate or low-risk patients, the risks of thrombolytic therapy outweigh the potential benefits and should only be used as a rescue therapy when hemodynamic decompensation occurs with anticoagulation alone. Anticoagulation regimens typically consist of an early dose of low-molecular-weight-heparin or fondaparinux, followed by a direct oral anticoagulant for a minimum of 3 mo. For those who may be considered for thrombolytic or other reperfusion therapies, an initial dose with a shorter half-life such as unfractionated heparin is preferred, and the unfractionated heparin is held while the patient receives thrombolytic therapy. For patients with intermediate or high-risk PE who are deemed to be candidates for thrombolytic therapy, systemic thrombolysis with recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (e.g. alteplase 100 mg) is most commonly used and administered under 2 h. In the case of failure or contraindications to thrombolysis, and in certain high-risk patients, surgical management may be necessary. Failure of thrombolytic therapy was defined by the AHA as progression of PE pathophysiology after administering systemic thrombolysis.
section_pearls: Norepinephrine is first-choice vasopressor; vasopressin is an adjunct.
VA-ECMO should be utilized early if prior interventions are not successful.
Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg over 2 h is the standard regimen for eligible high-risk PE patients.

sections.heading: Percutaneous Interventions for Thrombus Management
sections.content: At present, there are several catheter-directed therapies that have emerged in recent years as safe and effective options for the management of PE, including catheter-directed thrombolysis (CDT) and catheter-directed mechanical thrombectomy (MT). Among studies evaluating CDT, the ULTIMA (Ultrasound Accelerated Thrombolysis of Pulmonary Embolism) and HI-PEITHO trials stand out as randomized controlled trials demonstrating improved RV function and decreased PE-related death or cardiopulmonary complications compared to anticoagulation alone. Other trials such as SEATTLE-II, OPTALYSE PE, and CANARY also support these findings but provide lower level evidence. Similarly, for catheter-directed MT, the STORM-PE randomized controlled trial demonstrated decreased RV/LV ratio and earlier normalization of vital signs among intermediate-high risk PE patients when treated with MT compared to anticoagulation alone. Several other prospective, single-arm studies also demonstrate the safety of efficacy of MT in the management of intermediate or high risk PE. A brief summary of the major trials on CDT and MT for interventions for PE management, and the associated catheter type and access sites, were shown in Table 2. Recent data from the PEERLESS perspective, multicenter, randomized controlled trial provides additional insight into the expanded role of interventional approaches to PE management by providing a direct comparison of large-bore mechanical thrombectomy and CDT for intermediate-risk PE. In this study, large-bore mechanical thrombectomy demonstrated superior outcomes in a composite outcome including mortality, bleeding, and need for ICU admission compared to CDT, while maintaining comparable bleeding risks. Additional details highlighting these techniques are beyond the scope of this surgically-focused review.
section_pearls: HI-PEITHO trial: CDT plus anticoagulation showed 61% relative risk reduction in composite outcome of PE-related death, cardiopulmonary decompensation or collapse, or symptomatic PE recurrence within 7d compared to anticoagulation alone.
PEERLESS trial: Large-bore mechanical thrombectomy demonstrated superior composite outcomes (mortality, bleeding, ICU admission) compared to CDT for intermediate-risk PE.

sections.heading: Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation Support
sections.content:
VA-ECMO is also an intervention that may be considered in select cases of patients with PE. The AHA recommends use of VA-ECMO in patients with high risk PE and hemodynamic instability that does not improve with initial therapy. This includes persistent cardiogenic shock, cardiac arrest, or refractory hypoxemia. VA-ECMO can also be utilized to bridge high risk patients to interventional therapies. It also may be the only resort for patients undergoing active cardiopulmonary arrest receiving chest compressions. The role for ECMO in patients receiving thrombolytic and catheter-based therapy is both complex and evolving, and beyond the scope of this particular review. However, an understanding of the physiologic basis for ECMO utilization in the surgical PE patient is pertinent to our discussion and warrants outlining here.

VA-ECMO helps physiologically with both hypoxemia and hemodynamic collapse. Venous blood is diverted from the right heart to the ECMO circuit, which returns the oxygenated blood into the arterial system, thereby bypassing the obstructed pulmonary circulation and restoring systemic perfusion. This is beneficial in reducing the RV pressure overload and right heart dysfunction seen in patients with PE, which is why VA-ECMO is preferred over venous-venous ECMO. Most commonly, cannulation involves percutaneous or surgical access to the femoral vein and femoral artery. Alternative options include the internal jugular or subclavian vein for venous access, and the axillary or carotid artery for arterial access, although these are not typically used in urgent or emergent scenarios. Cannulation should be performed under ultrasound guidance along with transesophageal echocardiography or with the aid of fluoroscopy.

Broadly speaking, outcomes after use of VA-ECMO for PE are variable. Current data suggest that VA-ECMO does not improve short-term survival in patients with acute PE. However, limited data also suggest that VA-ECMO use in patients under 60 y of age or those who subsequently underwent surgical embolectomy have improved survival. Registry data from both the US and Germany have also shown that VA-ECMO used in adjunct with thrombolysis therapy (surgical or catheter-based)—reduced mortality. Importantly, the US registry analysis also demonstrated that the combination of VA-ECMO and reperfusion interventions yielded superior outcomes than VA-ECMO as a stand-alone therapy. However, the use of VA-ECMO is not without risk and must be used on a select basis. Complications of VA-ECMO include systemic inflammation, coagulopathies, stroke and seizures, acute kidney injury, pulmonary congestion, vascular injury, hemorrhage, and limb ischemia. Given these factors, it is still uncertain whether VA-ECMO should serve as purely a bridge to treatment in select cases or if there is value in its use as standalone therapy. Therefore, a thorough understanding of the risks and benefits of VA-ECMO use in PE is necessary to optimize the multidisciplinary care of these patients.

Unfortunately, other mechanical support platforms remain inadequate in the treatment of PE secondary to inadequate RV unloading. Veno-venous ECMO will only exacerbate RV distention by increased right-sided venous return. Furthermore, veno-venous ECMO is best utilized in scenarios of refractory hypoxia or hypercapnia which are typically not present in hemodynamically unstable patients with high risk PE. Other mechanical devices such as RV support devices (RVADs) placed either surgically or percutaneously have been reported, but little data exist to support their use. Regardless, these devices do not address the mechanical obstruction in the pulmonary arteries and can lead to increased RV afterload due to nonpulsatile axial flow in an already highly pressurized system. This can lead to subsequent RV wall tension and subendocardial ischemia. As such, these other platforms are not recommended at this time and additional studies are needed.
section_pearls: VA-ECMO is recommended by the AHA for high-risk PE with hemodynamic instability not improving with initial therapy.
US registry data showed the combination of VA-ECMO and reperfusion interventions yielded superior outcomes than VA-ECMO as stand-alone therapy.
Complications of VA-ECMO include systemic inflammation, coagulopathy, stroke, acute kidney injury, vascular injury, hemorrhage, and limb ischemia.

sections.heading: Historical Surgical Management
sections.content: Surgical pulmonary embolectomy has a long history dating back to the early 20th century with the work of Dr. Friedrich Trendelenburg. Trendelenburg's original operation was performed via left thoracotomy with tourniquet isolation of the proximal PA and aorta to occlude blood flow and enable visualization for pulmonary arteriotomy and clot extraction without devastating blood loss. An alternative early technique employed median sternotomy with circulatory arrest via venous inflow occlusion with clamping of the superior and inferior vena cava. Both early approaches, however, proved to have fundamental limitations. Occlusion of the PA, as performed in the Trendelenburg procedure, exacerbated right heart dysfunction by increasing afterload on an already tenuous RV. This accelerated RV failure and hemodynamic collapse, leading to the technique's dismal mortality rate of 87% in early reports. While avoiding direct PA occlusion, the venous inflow occlusion technique was similarly plagued by poor outcomes, as it eliminated preload to the heart thereby precipitating devastating hypotension and cardiac arrest. Compounding these technical drawbacks, the historically stringent indications for the surgical management of PE restricted its use to the sickest, most hemodynamically frail patients. Not surprisingly, both techniques were therefore riddled with failure and limited the use of surgery in acute PE management to select cases and centers.
section_pearls: Early Trendelenburg procedure carried an 87% mortality rate due to exacerbation of RV failure from PA occlusion.

sections.heading: Indications for Surgical Pulmonary Embolectomy
sections.content:
Following the advent of cardiopulmonary bypass, the successes of those select centers still performing pulmonary embolectomy demonstrated its role in management and reinvigorated what many regarded as a historical operation. Contemporary guidelines, such as those from the AHA and ESC, now recommend surgical embolectomy for centrally located high risk PE in cases where the following criteria are present:
- Fibrinolytics are contraindicated (e.g., current or prior intracranial hemorrhage, recent ischemic stroke (within 3 mo), active internal bleeding, and coagulopathy (INR > 1.7, platelet count <100,000, prothrombin time > 15sec, activated partial thromboplastin clotting time > 40sec).
- Systemic or catheter-based therapy has failed. Specifically, we consider failure of systemic therapy to include ongoing hemodynamic shock and instability, worsening RV failure, persistent clot burden, and clinical deterioration such as cardiac arrest or need for mechanical ventilation
- Clot-in-transit is present in the right heart or traversing a patent foramen ovale (PFO)
- A large PFO is present

Surgery may also be considered in certain cases of intermediate risk PE where there are signs of moderate or severe right heart dysfunction present, such as follows:
- RV/LV ratio >1.0 on imaging
- Concerning ECG changes (e.g., new right bundle branch block, T wave changes)
- Elevation of cardiac biomarkers (e.g., troponin, brain natriuretic peptide)
- Echocardiographic findings specific to acute right heart strain (e.g., McConnell's sign)

An overview of these indications for surgery in acute PE has been summarized in Table 3. In all cases where surgery is pursed, the pathologic thrombus burden should be centrally located in order to be surgically accessible and limit the risk of devastating vascular injury that can occur in the fragile distal vasculature. Importantly, assessment of chronicity is crucial for surgical planning, as underlying chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension would require a more complex pulmonary endarterectomy that is currently limited to experienced, high volume centers. In general, we anticipate the indications for surgical PE management will continue to evolve alongside the introduction of novel catheter-based modalities but nonetheless remain dependent on careful imaging and multidisciplinary evaluation.
section_pearls: Surgical embolectomy is indicated for centrally located high-risk PE when fibrinolytics are contraindicated, systemic or catheter-based therapy has failed, clot-in-transit is present, or a large PFO exists.

Contraindications to fibrinolytics include current or prior intracranial hemorrhage, recent ischemic stroke within 3 months, active internal bleeding, and coagulopathy (INR > 1.7, platelets <100,000, PT > 15 sec, aPTT > 40 sec).

sections.heading: Modern Surgical Technique
sections.content:
Here we will outline our conventional approach to the surgical management of acute PE. It should be noted that the following described technique is specific to the acute setting and distinct from the technical approach to acute on chronic PE in which pulmonary thromboendarterectomy is performed. We simplify the overall approach to surgical pulmonary embolectomy into the following major technical steps, each of which will be described in detail below and has numerous described iterations depending on the specific case and institution:
1. Median sternotomy and pericardiotomy
2. Establishment of cardiopulmonary bypass
3. Pulmonary arteriotomy and clot removal
4. Exploration of the right atrium and ventricle for thrombus (if present)
5. Consider closure of patent foramen ovale (if present)

Before the operation begins, careful evaluation of the patient's hemodynamic status is paramount. If indicated, percutaneous cardiopulmonary support should be utilized aggressively before the induction of anesthesia to avoid precipitous collapse. The operation itself begins with median sternotomy, followed by longitudinal pericardiotomy to provide ample exposure for central cannulation. Cannulation for cardiopulmonary bypass (CPB) is performed following systemic heparinization, typically with bicaval drainage of the superior vena cava and inferior vena cava and high ascending aortic cannulation. Alternatively, a femoral venous drainage cannula can be used instead of an inferior vena cava drainage cannula. After appropriate anticoagulation, CPB is established. Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) should be performed to evaluate ventricular size and function, look for possible thrombus in the right atrium or ventricle, and confirm there are no intracardiac defects such as a PFO or atrial septal defect. Moderate hypothermia may be established during CPB for cerebral protection, though the operation is commonly performed under normothermic conditions. Unless necessary or the repair of a known intracardiac defect (e.g., PFO) is planned; aortic cross clamping and cardioplegic arrest are commonly avoided in order to allow the RV to recover and contract in an unloaded state during the time of CPB perfusion. It should be noted that PFO closure in the setting of acute PE is a highly nuanced and complex decision that we evaluate on a case-by-case basis as a multidisciplinary team with consideration of specific patient history and intraoperative findings. PFO closure can precipitate hemodynamic collapse by eliminating a critical shunt for in a hemodynamically fragile heart. We therefore typically consider PFO closure in the specific scenario where large clot burden is actively straddling the PFO or observed in the left atrium to minimize the risk of systemic embolization and stroke. Routine snaring of both cavae can help ensure optimal visualization for thrombus removal by minimizing blood in the surgical field. Depending on the specific disease presentation and thrombus distribution, pulmonary arteriotomy can be performed via a single longitudinal incision on the main PA or via two incisions—one in the main PA with extension into the left PA and the second in the right main PA. Typically, the latter is performed. Thrombus is then removed from the arteriotomy, often with the assistance of various adjunct tools including suctioning, gallbladder stone forceps, retrograde perfusion for flushing via the pulmonary veins. Balloon tipped embolectomy catheters are generally not recommended due to risk of injury to thin distal PA vasculature. To ensure full removal of the thrombus, a bronchoscopy or portable camera can be inserted down PA branches to facilitate closer visualization and inspection. After removal of the thrombus, the pulmonary arteriotomy incisions are then closed typically with 5-0 or 6-0 prolene suture. Figure shows a representative image of a surgically removed centrally located, high risk pulmonary embolus.

If thrombus is identified in the right atrium or right ventricle, a right atriotomy may be performed for exploration and clot extraction. If necessary, this can also facilitate visualization of the junction between the inferior vena cava and the right atrium for additional clot removal. Clot in the right ventricle may be accessed through the tricuspid valve or through a main PA incision looking across the pulmonic valve. In rare circumstances, a right ventriculotomy can be performed to remove clot from the right ventricle. However, risks of injury to the pulmonic valve and right heart dysfunction must be considered. If there is concern for persistent thrombus within the pulmonary vasculature, additional adjunct operative techniques including retrograde flushing of the PA via the pulmonary veins have been described but are less commonly performed in our own experience.

After thrombus/clot has been removed from all locations, CPB is typically weaned. Typically, no deairing is required with right-sided cardiac surgery unless a PFO was repaired. In that instance, an aortic root vent needle should have been placed. After coming off CPB with adequate heart function and hemostasis, cannulae are removed and protamine is administered. Hemostasis is then policed. Chest tubes are then placed and the chest is then closed.
section_pearls: Aortic cross-clamping and cardioplegic arrest are commonly avoided to allow the RV to recover and contract in an unloaded state during CPB.
Balloon-tipped embolectomy catheters are generally not recommended due to risk of injury to thin distal PA vasculature.
Pulmonary arteriotomy incisions are typically closed with 5-0 or 6-0 prolene suture.

sections.heading: Surgical Outcomes and Complications
sections.content:
The historically poor outcomes of surgical pulmonary embolectomy are reflected in the low volume in the modern day. A retrospective review of the STS database from 2011 to 2015 reveals that of 1144 reporting centers, 310 performed at least one surgical pulmonary embolectomy during this period for a total of 1075 isolated acute embolectomies in patients without prior cardiac surgery. In contrast to early data, the operative mortality of these patients was 16%, however, the rate of major morbidity (defined as renal failure, new dialysis, prolonged ventilation, deep sternal infection, reoperation, or permanent stroke) neared 50%. These patients were stratified by acuity of PE from intermediate or high risk without cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and high risk with CPR. Those presenting with high risk PE had significantly greater rates of morbidity and mortality, ranging from 7.9% in the intermediate risk cohort to 44.4% in the cohort with high risk PE requiring CPR. The rates of major morbidities were significantly different between the high and intermediate risk cohorts, although there was no significant difference identified between high risk PE cohorts with and without CPR. In this study, the most common complication was prolonged ventilation (overall 44.5%).

A single center study in the same time period identified 55 patients stratified in similar fashion by clinical acuity. Their overall operative mortality was 7%, though patients with high risk PE with arrest had a mortality rate of 22%. Their overall 1-y survival was 91%, ranging from 100% in intermediate to 67% in high risk PE with arrest. In their patient population, RV dysfunction had almost completely normalized in all but one patient (2%), though they note this exception had signs of chronic thromboembolic disease. Their postoperative complications included prolonged intubation, tracheostomy, acute renal failure, pneumonia, deep sternal wound infection, and sepsis—their cohort had predominantly no significant differences in rates. Prolonged intubation was the most common complication and was significantly more likely in the cohort with high-risk PE and CPR at 88%, compared to the rate in intermediate risk PE of 25%.

An additional single center experience from 1999 to 2013 reviewed 115 patients through 3-y follow up and found excellent short-term survival in both their stable and unstable PE cohorts. Their overall mortality rate was 6.6%, with a differentiation between rates in patients with unstable 10.2% and stable 3.6% PE. The difference in survival increased through 3-y follow up, where they note a 65.8% and 80.4% survival rate for unstable and stable patients, respectively. However, there was no significant difference between the stable and unstable cohorts when comparing rates of in-hospital complications, which these authors defined as deep sternal wound infection, permanent stroke, new renal failure, coagulopathy, tamponade, sepsis, or recurrent PE. In these patients, the most common complication was the development of coagulopathy (overall 13%).

A meta-analysis of studies dating from 1965 to 2024 revealed an overall hospital mortality rate of 21.93%. Their identified complications included pulmonary bleeding (10.62%), gastrointestinal bleeding (4.7%), surgical site bleeding (4.84%), nonsurgical site bleeding (5.8%), and wound complications (7.2%). Though these data were not stratified by acuity of presentation, the publication year was found to be associated with significantly lower hospital mortality. Reflecting the above studies, the authors found that hemodynamic instability and preoperative cardiac arrest were associated with greater hospital mortality.

Overall, a review of contemporary data demonstrates that surgical pulmonary embolectomy has acceptable rates of morbidity and mortality. While there is a dearth of long-term outcomes for these patients, both in-hospital and short-term outcomes show significant improvement from historical data that have limited the widespread acceptance of surgical management for acute PE. A summary of the studies elucidating outcomes after surgical embolectomy was shown in Table 4.
section_pearls: STS database review (2011-2015): operative mortality 16%, major morbidity near 50%, most common complication prolonged ventilation (44.5%).
Single center study (2011-2015): operative mortality 7%, 1-y survival 91%, RV dysfunction normalized in 98% of patients.
Meta-analysis (pre-2014): overall hospital mortality 21.93%, with hemodynamic instability and preoperative cardiac arrest associated with greater mortality.

sections.heading: Future Directions
sections.content: Currently, there are no randomized controlled trials comparing surgical embolectomy with other management options. Current recommendations are consensus-based rather than founded upon data from randomized trials. Available studies comparing surgical embolectomy to other options are retrospective in nature and have inherent patient selection biases. As the volume of pulmonary embolectomies have only increased in recent years, analysis of long term follow up data would also provide valuable insight into the efficacy of surgery compared to other management strategies. Lastly, there is currently substantial variation in surgical indication across institutions, with some employing surgical embolectomy as the primary treatment while others reserve the operation for salvage therapy. Future efforts to create standardized indications for surgical embolectomy are necessary.

sections.heading: Conclusions
sections.content: Today, contemporary management of PE remains nuanced and is dictated by the careful coordination of multidisciplinary teams and guided by widely accepted consensus guidelines. While medical management remains the cornerstone of PE treatment, improved outcomes in contemporary data support the role for surgery in the treatment of patients with large, centrally located disease who have failed or are not candidates for thrombolysis or transcatheter thrombectomy. As outlined in this review, surgery for acute PE has gone from being a nearly extinct historical operation to what is now an increasingly accepted modality in the overall algorithm of standard PE management.

strengths_limitations: Strengths: Provides a comprehensive, surgically-focused overview of contemporary interventional and surgical management of acute PE, including detailed modern surgical technique descriptions and a summary of major trials. Limitations: Narrative review without systematic literature search; no randomized controlled trials comparing surgical embolectomy to other management options exist; current recommendations are consensus-based; available comparative studies are retrospective with inherent selection biases.
